title: Anxiety is not the enemy
subtitle: How can we help our children grow and learn?
paragraphs: Parenting is a strange thing. It goes on for ever, but also passes in a flash. It’s hard to see change, and yet suddenly your baby is taking the bus by themselves. You often have no idea whether you’re ‘doing it right’ and whether they’ll thank you or hate you for it later.
Parenting pushes us out of our comfort zone. Our children challenge us in ways that we could never have imagined before they arrived. They stretch us, sometimes beyond what we think we can manage.

And it’s a constantly changing game. Just as you think you’ve got something sorted, something else comes alone. One child is more settled, and the other ones start to struggle. They grow, and each new stage resolves some issues and brings new challenges.

I think of growing up as a process of expanding comfort zones, and that can be an uncomfortable process. As children become more capable, they need new challenges and opportunities. That’s the balance that we’re treading with them. One step forward, dip a toe into something new – and then it goes wrong and we’re back, making sense of what happened. We’re balancing safety and challenge, all the time. Try something out, see how it goes – and then try again. They can’t stay in their comfort zone, because they are growing and they need challenge for that.

All of this creates anxiety, and that’s okay. Anxiety isn’t our enemy. It’s part of life, our warning system that keeps us safe. Without anxiety we’d run across the road without looking. We’d touch hot stoves and approach wild animals. We need our anxiety.

So what goes wrong? Sometimes that anxiety is too much to bear, and sometimes the challenge can overwhelm children’s (and adult’s) capacity to cope. Sometimes we become scared of anxiety, and we try to make it go away. We avoid all the triggers, and the world gets smaller. The irony is, the more we try to make the anxiety go away, the more likely it is to come back. We can’t get rid of our feelings.

Parenting is about balancing safety and challenge. Children try something new, and sometimes it goes wrong. Sometimes it doesn’t. Either way, we are there beside them, showing them that we can face the world together even when we feel anxious.
Anxiety is not our enemy. It’s more like a cautious (and sometimes annoying) friend.
